category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part C/Abstract Algebra — Polynomial Rings and Irreducibility

questiontext.text:
Consider \(p(X)=X^3+nX+1\in\mathbb{Z}[X]\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(p(X)\) is reducible over \(\mathbb{Z}\) for \(n=-2\) (since \(p(1)=0\)).
  2. \(p(X)\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Z}\) for infinitely many \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\).
  3. If \(p(X)\) is reducible over \(\mathbb{Z}\), it has a rational root by the rational root theorem.
  4. \(p(X)\) is always irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) for \(n>0\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) \(p(1)=1+n+1=n+2=0\Rightarrow n=-2\). (B) By Eisenstein or direct argument, infinitely many \(n\) give irreducible polynomials (e.g. \(n=0\): \(X^3+1=(X+1)(X^2-X+1)\) — reducible; \(n=1\): \(X^3+X+1\) has no rational roots \(\pm1\) (check: \(1+1+1=3\ne0\), \(-1-1+1=-1\ne0\)) — irreducible). (C) A cubic over \(\mathbb{Z}\) is reducible iff it has a linear factor, i.e. a rational root. (D) FALSE: \(n=0\) gives \(X^3+1\) which factors.

questiontext.text:
Eisenstein's criterion: if \(p\) is prime and \(f(X)=a_nX^n+\cdots+a_0\in\mathbb{Z}[X]\) with \(p\nmid a_n\), \(p\mid a_i\) for \(i

  1. \(X^p-p\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) for any prime \(p\).
  2. \(X^4+4\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) (apply Eisenstein with \(p=2\)).
  3. The \(p\)-th cyclotomic polynomial \(\Phi_p(X)=(X^p-1)/(X-1)\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
  4. Eisenstein can be applied after a substitution \(X\mapsto X+a\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (C), (D)

(A) \(X^p-p\): Eisenstein with the prime \(p\): \(p mid1\), \(p\mid(-p)\), \(p^2 mid(-p)\) (since \(p^2 mid p\) for prime \(p\)). Irreducible. (B) FALSE: \(X^4+4=(X^2+2X+2)(X^2-2X+2)\) — Sophie Germain identity. (C) \(\Phi_p(X)=X^{p-1}+\cdots+1\); substituting \(X+1\) and applying Eisenstein with prime \(p\). (D) The substitution \(X\mapsto X+a\) preserves irreducibility; Eisenstein can sometimes be applied after this.

questiontext.text:
Which are TRUE?

  1. Every PID (principal ideal domain) is a UFD.
  2. \(\mathbb{Z}\) is a PID.
  3. \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\) is a UFD but NOT a PID.
  4. Every field is a PID.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) PID \(\Rightarrow\) UFD (fundamental theorem). (B) \(\mathbb{Z}\) has all ideals principal: \((n)\). (C) \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\) is a UFD (Gauss's lemma + \(\mathbb{Z}\) UFD), but not a PID: \((2,X)\) is not principal. (D) In a field, every ideal is \((0)\) or \((1)\), both principal.

questiontext.text:
Let \(R\) be a commutative ring with unity and \(I\) an ideal. Which are TRUE?

  1. \(I\) is maximal iff \(R/I\) is a field.
  2. \(I\) is prime iff \(R/I\) is an integral domain.
  3. Every maximal ideal is prime.
  4. Every prime ideal is maximal (in a PID).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A)(B) Standard characterizations. (C) Fields are integral domains, so maximal \(\Rightarrow\) prime. (D) FALSE in general: \((0)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\) is prime but not maximal. In a PID, every nonzero prime ideal is maximal, but \((0)\) may be prime without being maximal.

questiontext.text:
Gauss's Lemma: a primitive polynomial in \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\) that factors over \(\mathbb{Q}\) factors over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Which follow?

  1. If \(f\in\mathbb{Z}[X]\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Z}\) and primitive, then it is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
  2. \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\) is a UFD.
  3. The product of primitive polynomials is primitive.
  4. An irreducible element of \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\) is either a prime integer or an irreducible primitive polynomial.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Converse of Gauss's lemma. (B) \(\mathbb{Z}\) is UFD and \(F[X]\) for field \(F\) is a PID hence UFD; by Gauss's lemma, \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\) is UFD. (C) Gauss's lemma content: content of product = product of contents; primitive × primitive = primitive. (D) UFD structure of \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\): irreducibles are primes and irreducible primitives.

questiontext.text:
Which are TRUE about irreducible polynomials over \(\mathbb{F}_p\)?

  1. For each \(n\ge1\), there exists an irreducible polynomial of degree \(n\) over \(\mathbb{F}_p\).
  2. The number of monic irreducible polynomials of degree \(n\) over \(\mathbb{F}_p\) is \(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{d|n}\mu(n/d)p^d\) (Necklace formula).
  3. \(\mathbb{F}_p[X]/(f(X))\) is a field iff \(f\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{F}_p\).
  4. Every polynomial over \(\mathbb{F}_p\) splits completely over \(\mathbb{F}_{p^{n!}}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}\) exists and is generated by roots of degree-\(n\) irreducibles. (B) Möbius inversion formula for counting irreducibles. (C) \(\mathbb{F}_p[X]/(f)\) is a field iff \(f\) irreducible (maximal ideal). (D) Every finite extension of \(\mathbb{F}_p\) embeds in \(\mathbb{F}_{p^{n!}}\) for large enough \(n\).

questiontext.text:
Rational Root Theorem: if \(f(X)=a_nX^n+\cdots+a_0\in\mathbb{Z}[X]\) has a rational root \(p/q\) (in lowest terms), then \(p\mid a_0\) and \(q\mid a_n\). Which follow?

  1. A monic polynomial in \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\) has only integer rational roots.
  2. To check irreducibility of a cubic over \(\mathbb{Q}\), it suffices to check for rational roots.
  3. \(X^3-2\) has no rational roots.
  4. If \(f\in\mathbb{Z}[X]\) has no rational roots, \(f\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) Monic: \(a_n=1\), so \(q\mid1\Rightarrow q=\pm1\Rightarrow p/q=p\in\mathbb{Z}\). (B) A cubic over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is irreducible iff it has no rational roots (else it factors as linear × quadratic). (C) Possible rational roots of \(X^3-2\): \(\pm1,\pm2\); none are roots. (D) FALSE: a degree-4 polynomial with no rational roots may still factor into two irreducible quadratics, e.g. \(X^4+4=(X^2+2X+2)(X^2-2X+2)\).

questiontext.text:
The \(n\)-th cyclotomic polynomial \(\Phi_n(X)=\prod_{1\le k\le n,\gcd(k,n)=1}(X-e^{2\pi ik/n})\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(X^n-1=\prod_{d|n}\Phi_d(X)\).
  2. \(\deg\Phi_n=\phi(n)\) (Euler's totient function).
  3. \(\Phi_n(X)\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) for all \(n\).
  4. \(\Phi_p(X)=X^{p-1}+X^{p-2}+\cdots+X+1\) for prime \(p\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Standard factorization of \(X^n-1\). (B) \(\phi(n)\) primitive roots of unity. (C) All cyclotomic polynomials are irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) — classical theorem. (D) For prime \(p\): \(\Phi_p=(X^p-1)/(X-1)\).

questiontext.text:
Let \(\phi:R\to S\) be a ring homomorphism. Which are TRUE?

  1. \(\ker\phi\) is an ideal of \(R\).
  2. \(\mathrm{Im}(\phi)\) is a subring of \(S\).
  3. First isomorphism: \(R/\ker\phi\cong\mathrm{Im}(\phi)\).
  4. If \(\phi\) is surjective and \(R\) is a field, then \(\phi\) is injective (hence an isomorphism).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A)(B)(C) Standard ring isomorphism theorems. (D) If \(R\) is a field, its only ideals are \((0)\) and \(R\); since \(\phi\) is surjective (onto \(S\ne\{0\}\)), \(\ker\phi\ne R\), so \(\ker\phi=(0)\Rightarrow\phi\) is injective.

questiontext.text:
In \(F[X]\) for a field \(F\), the division algorithm states: for \(f,g\in F[X]\) with \(g\ne0\), \(\exists!q,r\in F[X]\) with \(f=gq+r\) and \(\deg r<\deg g\). Which follow?

  1. \(F[X]\) is a Euclidean domain.
  2. \(F[X]\) is a PID.
  3. Every ideal in \(F[X]\) is generated by the GCD of its elements.
  4. The GCD of two polynomials over \(F\) can be computed by the Euclidean algorithm.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) The Euclidean norm \(\deg f\) gives \(F[X]\) a Euclidean algorithm. (B) Euclidean domain \(\Rightarrow\) PID. (C)(D) In PIDs, ideals are principal and generated by GCDs; the Euclidean algorithm computes GCDs.

name.text: Poly - Extension Fields and Minimal Polynomial

questiontext.text:
Let \(\alpha\) be algebraic over \(F\) with minimal polynomial \(m_\alpha\in F[X]\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(m_\alpha\) is irreducible over \(F\).
  2. \(F(\alpha)\cong F[X]/(m_\alpha)\).
  3. \([F(\alpha):F]=\deg m_\alpha\).
  4. Any polynomial in \(F[X]\) with \(\alpha\) as a root is divisible by \(m_\alpha\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Minimal polynomial is irreducible by minimality and the fact that \(F[X]/(m_\alpha)\) is a field. (B) \(F(\alpha)\cong F[X]/(m_\alpha)\) (evaluation homomorphism with kernel \((m_\alpha)\)). (C) Degree of extension = degree of minimal polynomial. (D) \(m_\alpha\) divides any polynomial vanishing at \(\alpha\) (since \((m_\alpha)\) is maximal hence prime).

questiontext.text:
If \(f\in\mathbb{Z}[X]\) reduces to an irreducible polynomial over \(\mathbb{F}_p\) (for some prime \(p\) not dividing the leading coefficient), then \(f\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\). Which are TRUE?

  1. This provides a sufficient condition for irreducibility over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
  2. If \(f\) reduces mod \(p\) to a product of irreducibles of distinct degrees, those degrees constrain the Galois group.
  3. If \(f\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{F}_p\) for every prime \(p\), it is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
  4. The converse is FALSE: an irreducible \(f\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) may be reducible mod every prime.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (D)

(A) Reduction mod \(p\) criterion for irreducibility. (B) The cycle type of Frobenius elements gives information about the Galois group. (C) FALSE: e.g. \(X^4-10X^2+1\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) but reducible mod every prime. (D) TRUE: such polynomials exist — this shows the reduction criterion is not "if and only if".

questiontext.text:
A ring \(R\) is Noetherian if every ascending chain of ideals stabilizes. Which are TRUE?

  1. Every PID is Noetherian.
  2. If \(R\) is Noetherian, so is \(R[X]\) (Hilbert basis theorem).
  3. \(\mathbb{Z}[X_1,X_2,\ldots]\) (infinitely many variables) is NOT Noetherian.
  4. Every subring of a Noetherian ring is Noetherian.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) PIDs are Noetherian (every ideal is principal, hence finitely generated). (B) Hilbert basis theorem. (C) The ideal \((X_1,X_2,\ldots)\) is not finitely generated, so the ring is not Noetherian. (D) FALSE: a subring of a Noetherian ring need not be Noetherian. For example, a polynomial ring over a Noetherian ring has subrings that are not Noetherian.

questiontext.text:
If \(F\subseteq K\subseteq L\) are fields, the tower law states \([L:F]=[L:K][K:F]\). Which follow?

  1. \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]=4\).
  2. If \([K:F]=p\) (prime), there are no intermediate fields between \(F\) and \(K\).
  3. \([\mathbb{C}:\mathbb{Q}]\) is infinite.
  4. \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=3\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2,\sqrt3):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2)]=2\) and \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2):\mathbb{Q}]=2\), so total = 4. (B) If \([K:F]=p\), any intermediate field \(F\subset E\subset K\) would have \(p=[K:F]=[K:E][E:F]\), forcing \([K:E]=1\) or \([E:F]=1\). (C) \(\mathbb{C}\) is uncountable over \(\mathbb{Q}\). (D) Minimal polynomial of \(\sqrt[3]{2}\) is \(X^3-2\), degree 3.

questiontext.text:
Let \(K/F\) be a Galois extension with Galois group \(G=\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(|G|=[K:F]\).
  2. The fundamental theorem: there is a bijection between subgroups of \(G\) and intermediate fields, reversing inclusion.
  3. A subextension \(E/F\) is Galois iff the corresponding subgroup is normal in \(G\).
  4. If \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\), then \(K/F\) has a unique subfield of each degree dividing \(n\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(|G|=[K:F]\) for a Galois extension. (B) Fundamental theorem of Galois theory. (C) \(E/F\) Galois \(\Leftrightarrow\mathrm{Gal}(K/E)\trianglelefteq G\). (D) Cyclic Galois group: unique subgroup for each divisor \(\Rightarrow\) unique intermediate field.

questiontext.text:
A polynomial is solvable by radicals iff its Galois group is solvable. Which are TRUE?

  1. All polynomials of degree \(\le4\) are solvable by radicals.
  2. There exist polynomials of degree 5 not solvable by radicals.
  3. A generic polynomial of degree \(n\ge5\) has Galois group \(S_n\), which is not solvable.
  4. The Abel-Ruffini theorem states no general formula for degree \(\ge5\) polynomial solutions exists using radicals.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Galois groups of degree \(\le4\) polynomials are solvable groups (\(S_4\) is solvable). (B) e.g. \(X^5-5X+12\) has Galois group \(S_5\) — not solvable. (C) The "generic" polynomial of degree \(n\) has Galois group \(S_n\). (D) Abel-Ruffini theorem (proved rigorously by Galois theory).

questiontext.text:
A real number \(\alpha\) is constructible iff \([\mathbb{Q}(\alpha):\mathbb{Q}]\) is a power of 2. Which are TRUE?

  1. \(\sqrt{2}\) is constructible.
  2. \(\sqrt[3]{2}\) is not constructible (doubling the cube is impossible).
  3. \(\cos(2\pi/17)\) is constructible (regular 17-gon is constructible).
  4. \(\pi\) is constructible.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt2):\mathbb{Q}]=2=2^1\). (B) \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=3\) (not a power of 2). (C) Gauss: 17 is a Fermat prime \((17=2^4+1)\), so \(\cos(2\pi/17)\) is constructible. (D) FALSE: \(\pi\) is transcendental, hence not algebraic at all, so not constructible.

name.text: Poly - Chinese Remainder Theorem for Rings

questiontext.text:
CRT for rings: if \(I,J\trianglelefteq R\) with \(I+J=R\), then \(R/(I\cap J)\cong R/I\times R/J\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(\mathbb{Z}/(mn)\cong\mathbb{Z}/m\times\mathbb{Z}/n\) when \(\gcd(m,n)=1\).
  2. CRT for polynomial rings: \(F[X]/(fg)\cong F[X]/(f)\times F[X]/(g)\) when \(\gcd(f,g)=1\).
  3. \(I\cap J=IJ\) when \(I+J=R\).
  4. The isomorphism in CRT is a ring isomorphism, not just a group isomorphism.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(\gcd(m,n)=1\Rightarrow m\mathbb{Z}+n\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{Z}\). (B) Coprime polynomials give the CRT. (C) When \(I+J=R\): \(IJ\subseteq I\cap J\) always; for the other inclusion: \(x\in I\cap J\Rightarrow x=x\cdot1=x(i+j)=xi+xj\in IJ\). (D) The CRT map is a ring isomorphism.

questiontext.text:
A commutative ring \(R\) is an integral domain if it has no zero divisors. Which are TRUE?

  1. Every field is an integral domain.
  2. Every integral domain can be embedded in its field of fractions.
  3. \(\mathbb{Z}[i]=\{a+bi:a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\}\) is an integral domain.
  4. \(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}\) is an integral domain.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) Fields have no zero divisors. (B) Field of fractions construction (analogous to \(\mathbb{Q}\) from \(\mathbb{Z}\)). (C) \(\mathbb{Z}[i]\) is a subring of \(\mathbb{C}\), hence an integral domain (in fact a PID, Gaussian integers). (D) FALSE: \(2\cdot3=6\equiv0\pmod6\) with \(2,3\ne0\) — zero divisors exist.

questiontext.text:
A module \(M\) over a ring \(R\) generalises a vector space (with \(R\) not necessarily a field). Which are TRUE?

  1. Every vector space is a module over its scalar field.
  2. Every abelian group is a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module.
  3. A free \(R\)-module has a basis (a linearly independent spanning set).
  4. Over a PID, every finitely generated module is a direct sum of cyclic modules.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Vector spaces are modules with the scalar field. (B) Abelian groups have a unique \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module structure: \(n\cdot a=a+a+\cdots+a\). (C) Free modules have bases by definition. (D) Structure theorem for modules over PIDs: \(M\cong R^r\oplus R/(d_1)\oplus\cdots\oplus R/(d_k)\).
